description: While a bridge remains damaged, neighbours may hope that it can reopen before winter. 'soliru' holds a desired outcome as possible while its result remains uncertain. 'pula' (wish) says only that the outcome would be welcome, 'norethi' (confident) reports strength of judgement, and 'seru' (commit) binds someone to a course of action.
articulatory_notes: Keep the tongue blade close to the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 's' and release the air into true-mid back rounded 'o'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l' and move into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Make a tap or short trill for 'r', then raise the back of the tongue and round the lips for high-back 'u'.
usage_notes: A hoped-for thing can be the direct object of 'soliru'; a complete outcome takes the 'mena ... meno' frame. Used nominally, 'soliru' is hope or an act of hoping. 'wiroa' describes someone or something as hopeful without repeating the event verb.

examples.phi: mia mena sila towe so nai meno soliru.
examples.translation: I hope that the community will be well.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A neighbourhood may hope for a repaired watershed while still naming the labour, evidence, and uncertain conditions involved. Hope keeps a possible future in view; it does not claim that goodwill has built it.
pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Hope and uncertainty can remain in the same sentence. That leaves room for a desired outcome without turning attachment to it into certainty.

semantic_domains.cognition: regarding a desired but unresolved outcome as possible
semantic_domains.emotion: felt orientation towards an outcome one would welcome
